video_title: The Great Private Equity Hangover | Protect the Pile Episode 19 w/ Dan Rasmussen
gist: Verdad Capital's Dan Rasmussen argues private equity is a leveraged-microcap strategy whose 15 years of no mark-to-market taught allocators nothing about risk, and that the 'great hangover' will play out slowly via private-credit-on-private-equity restructurings rather than a sudden panic. He remains bullish on cheap Japanese small caps post-2023 governance reform, sees the yen due for a rebound, favors US mid/small caps, and offers a contrarian AI thesis in which small companies capture the margin upside and large-cap AI capex disappoints.

themes.theme: PE as leveraged microcaps with no risk learning

insights.claim: Private equity is functionally a leveraged microcap strategy whose returns have recently trailed public equity, but the illiquid marks ('mark-to-mark-to-make-believe') mask both the leverage and the underperformance from allocators.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Investors should treat PE exposure as levered small/microcap beta and haircut reported IRRs for the absence of true mark-to-market.
insights.quote: as bad as the returns have been recently, and they're not all that bad cuz the marks, you know, it's mark-to-mark-to-make-believe, but they've been worse than public equity

insights.claim: Because private equity funds have gone roughly 15 years without a down quarter, GPs and allocators never learned risk management and instead simply escalated risk-taking, unlike public small-cap managers hardened by daily mark-to-market.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The absence of Darwinian selection in PE implies latent, unhedged risk that only surfaces in a forced-marking event.
insights.quote: imagine if your fund has never gone down. You've had 15 years not a down down quarter. Have you learned anything? I really think they haven't. They've just learned to take more risk.

insights.claim: The number of private equity funds has risen roughly 6x over the past 15 years, and there are now more private equity funds in the US than there are McDonald's locations.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Explosive supply of PE vehicles signals overcapitalization and crowding that compresses future returns.
insights.quote: there are more private equity funds in this country than there are McDonald's

insights.claim: Private equity's risk peaked in 2021-2022 during a 'software-palooza' when firms bought software companies at roughly 25x EBITDA and 12 turns of leverage, seeding the current 'great hangover.'
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Vintage 2021-2022 PE software deals are the most likely epicenter of write-downs and restructurings.
insights.quote: that peaked in '21, '22 with this like software palooza where they just bought every software company known to man for like 25 times EBITDA or 12 turns of leverage

themes.theme: Private credit as the forcing function

insights.claim: Private equity is structurally subordinate to private credit, so if private credit marks down 30%, private equity mathematically has to be worth even less, yet PE has drawn roughly one-tenth the negative attention private credit has.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Watch private-credit marks and BDC discounts as the leading indicator for larger, still-unrecognized PE equity losses.
insights.quote: private equity is subordinate to private credit. Like, it has to be worse. Like a private credit is down 30, private equity has to be down more.

insights.claim: Publicly traded BDCs trading down ~30% while privately marked BDCs show no decline is the observable NAV-vs-market gap that first exposed private credit, a pressure private equity has not yet faced.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The BDC discount is the tradable public proxy for the private-market marking gap; a similar reckoning in PE vehicles is still ahead.
insights.quote: you started to see the BDCs trade to home... if this publicly traded BDC is down 30% and you're telling me that my privately traded BDC is not down at all... seems like something's wrong

insights.claim: The PE unwind will be a slow-rolling crisis rather than a panic because nothing forces marks, and it will resolve via rising 'private credit on private equity, sponsor on sponsor violence' with private credit firms ending up owning many portfolio companies after bankruptcy.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Position for distressed control transfers to credit funds rather than a single dislocation; the value only unlocks post-bankruptcy.
insights.quote: I think what we're going to see is a lot more private credit on private equity, you know, sponsor on sponsor violence. I think we're just in the beginning stages of that

insights.claim: Annual-recurring-revenue (ARR) loans, a key innovation that fueled private credit by treating recurring revenue as collateral for asset-light software companies, are now revealing why that underwriting was flawed as software valuations and the AI narrative pressure the borrowers.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: ARR-backed leveraged loans on 2021-era software LBOs are a prime candidate for impairment as revenue durability is questioned.
insights.quote: how do you lend to companies with no assets? And they came up with what you were describing this annual recurring revenue loan... well, now we find out why that was dumb

insights.claim: The PE roll-up model has degraded from a real arbitrage (buying family businesses at ~5x EBITDA and taking them public at 6-8x) into paying inflated multiples for tiny, low-margin, high-bankruptcy-rate businesses like dental, veterinary, lawn care and dry cleaners that have no economies of scale.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Micro-cap roll-up funds are buying manufactured 'scale' with no real synergies, implying the pro-forma multiple arbitrage is illusory.
insights.quote: 50 dry cleaners is not just as good as Google, right? It's just 50 dry cleaners.

insights.claim: Roll-up sellers are now getting inbound offers of 14-16x EBITDA for businesses like pest control that historically sold at ~5-8x, with buyers justifying it via pro-forma 'we're only paying eight times' math and comps to public peers.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kenny
insights.so_what: Entry multiples on micro-cap roll-ups have inflated to levels that only work under pro-forma consolidation assumptions, a classic late-cycle tell.
insights.quote: he's gotten inbound calls for like 14 times EBITDA, 16 times EBITDA to buy this business, and he's just like, "What are they talking about?"

insights.claim: Stale private marks are extreme: Beyond Meat is reportedly still carried at roughly a $10 valuation inside private equity despite the public equity's market cap collapsing to well under a couple hundred million, illustrating marks that haven't reset in years.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kenny
insights.so_what: Continuation vehicles are warehousing losses that must eventually be realized; large gaps between private marks and public comps flag write-down risk.
insights.quote: the Beyond Burger is still trading at like it's still marked at like $10 valuation like inside of private equity. So it hasn't taken a market in like years.

insights.claim: The 'hottest new thing' in private equity fundraising is buying and rolling up old-school businesses (car washes, laundromats, pool cleaning) with a pitch to use AI to increase margins, which the panel views as a late-cycle marketing gimmick.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: 'AI-enhanced' roll-up funds are the newest fundraising narrative; treat AI-margin claims on trivial businesses as red flags.
insights.quote: the hottest new thing in private equity fundraising is exactly that. We're going to go buy and roll up old school businesses and then use AI to increase margins. I kid you not, this is so hot right now.

insights.claim: Institutional allocators like college endowments show zero skepticism toward private equity, while self-directed high-net-worth retail investors ask the basic liquidity and edge questions ('why does it outperform', 'can I get my money out') that PE marketers are poorly prepared to answer.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The smart-money/dumb-money framing is inverted here; retail skepticism plus PE's push into retail channels could be the pressure point that forces marks.
insights.quote: if you talk to people that work at supposedly the great institutions... they have zero skepticism about private equity. Zero.

insights.claim: PE and private credit firms including KKR and Apollo are aggressively gathering assets through the retail channel via liquid alts and dedicated conference desks, and mixing illiquid investments into liquid vehicles is the vulnerability that forces marks once retail redemptions begin.
insights.speaker: John McNamara
insights.so_what: Retail-facing semi-liquid PE/credit products (evoking BREIT-style gates) are the most likely trigger for forced selling and mark resets.
insights.quote: Do not mix illiquid investments with a liquid vehicle. That becomes the last gasp for of the desperate and like the first knife in your back

insights.claim: Private equity is only about 5% the size of the public equity market, roughly the size of India's stock market and available at similar or slightly lower fee-adjusted cost, so a 30-40% endowment allocation to PE is as arbitrary as putting 40% in India because 'it's the best performing market.'
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Market-cap-weight anchoring exposes PE allocations as wildly oversized relative to the opportunity set's true scale.
insights.quote: private equity is like, maybe 5% the size of the public equity market... It's roughly the size of India.

insights.claim: The dominant PE outperformance narrative echoes the emerging-markets consensus of ~15 years ago ('put all your money in it because it outperforms'), a circular justification that proved false for EM and is likely to prove false for PE.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Past-performance-chasing into a small, illiquid asset class rhymes with prior consensus blowups; fade the 'it outperforms because it outperforms' logic.
insights.quote: it's like we used to think that about emerging markets 15 years ago

insights.claim: Japan's debt-to-GDP problem is overstated because the Bank of Japan holds large assets, much of it invested in the Japanese equity market, and the country has been paying down debt, making net debt 'really not material anymore.'
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The consensus 'huge debt-to-GDP' bear case on Japan ignores the asset side; net-debt framing supports a more constructive view.
insights.quote: their like net debt problem is really not material anymore

insights.claim: Japanese small caps are extraordinarily cheap and improving thanks to corporate governance reform beginning in 2023 that pressures companies to raise dividends, do buybacks and stop hoarding cash, making Japan a 'cheap with a catalyst' value setup.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Focus on the most inefficient Japanese small caps (e.g. half of market cap in cash) as targets for governance-driven re-rating.
insights.quote: I specialize in sort of the most inefficient... stuff that has like half their market cap in cash... and betting that that's going to change

insights.claim: Rising Japanese interest rates should be read as bullish, not bearish, because rates track nominal GDP and their rise signals Japan has finally escaped its deflationary spiral into positive inflation and nominal growth.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Fade the fear that rising JGB yields hurt Japanese equities; reframe them as confirmation of reflation, which is healthy for the economy.
insights.quote: I actually think the rising rates, which I think a lot of people are worried about, um, are a good thing

insights.claim: Japan's demographic decline is real but its export-driven 'national champion' companies (e.g. Nintendo, Tokyo Electron) built on fanatical focus on excellence can thrive despite 1-2% GDP, mirroring how South Korea's similarly poor demographics haven't stopped strong equity returns.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Own globally competitive Japanese exporters where product niche outweighs domestic demographic drag; demographics alone aren't a sell signal.

insights.claim: Roughly 20% of the Japanese equity market is auto or auto-related and faces real threats from Chinese competition and EVs, an uncertain overhang on that large industry.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The auto exposure is a concentrated structural risk to Japanese indices; favor non-auto national champions to avoid Chinese-EV disruption.
insights.quote: probably 20% of the market there is auto or auto related

insights.claim: The yen is poised for strengthening because hedge fund and futures positioning is at extreme bearish levels, a non-consensus view driven by positioning rather than fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Crowded short positioning sets up an asymmetric long-yen trade; watch for a positioning-driven squeeze.
insights.quote: all sort of futures market positioning is so, so bearish on the yen. Like it's just so extreme right now. I think we're ready. I think we're poised for some sort of rebound.

insights.claim: Recent yen weakness stems from the carry trade (borrowing in yen to invest in higher-rate markets) plus an oil-price shock that made other developed markets unlikely to cut rates, removing Japan's relative-tightener advantage.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The yen's weakness is rate-differential and carry-driven, so a shift in relative central-bank paths is the catalyst to watch for a reversal.

themes.theme: US small/mid caps and bubble signals

insights.claim: US small-cap and microcap stocks have been 'on fire' in 2026, benefiting disproportionately from a post-Iran-ceasefire high-beta 'low beta junk rally' with 98th-99th percentile moves in high-beta, high-volatility and heavily-shorted baskets.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: The junk rally has torqued quant long-short books (long mid-cap quality, short small-cap junk); the factor rotation is a live risk and opportunity.
insights.quote: since the ceasefire in Iran was announced, you've seen this bat massive uh, low beta junk rally. Massive.

insights.claim: US mid-cap stocks are trading roughly in line with their long-term average valuation and are 'dirt cheap' relative to large caps, meaning headline high CAPE ratios are driven by mega-caps while the market's middle is neglected.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kenny
insights.so_what: Mid caps offer valuation support absent in mega-caps; a rotation out of the barbell (loss-making small growth + mega-cap) could favor the middle.
insights.quote: mid caps were like dirt cheap compared to and and actually on basically the same as their long-term average valuation

insights.claim: Massive equity issuance is one of the final stages of a bubble, and the recent wave of deals (including SpaceX-style offerings) signals the market is now in that phase, prompting real worry.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: Rising equity-issuance volume is a late-cycle warning; heavy new-deal supply is a signal to reduce risk.
insights.quote: one of the sort of final stages of a bubble is massive equity issuance

insights.claim: Small-cap vs large-cap relative performance tends to swing in roughly 15-year cycles over 80 years of history, and after ~15 years of small-cap underperformance and a strong recent run, this could mark the beginning of a new small-cap leadership regime.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kenny
insights.so_what: If the ~15-year cycle framework holds, size-factor allocation may be at an inflection favoring small over large.

insights.claim: In Rasmussen's low-conviction contrarian scenario, AI proves better at sounding smart to smart people than doing real work, so large-cap AI capex delivers far lower ROI and revenue than expected while small companies capture the margin upside by using 'good enough' AI to cut costs, torquing performance toward small caps.
insights.speaker: Dan Rasmussen
insights.so_what: A bearish-large-cap/bullish-small-cap AI outcome is an under-priced tail; watch AI capex ROI and small-company margin expansion as the tell.
insights.quote: AI turns out to be better at sounding smart to smart people than it is at actually doing anything

insights.claim: Large-cap incumbents face high AI switching costs (like 'building a new subway in Rome') while small caps can adopt existentially and leapfrog, analogous to Kenya having better 5G than the US because it never buried copper.
insights.speaker: Brooks Cutright
insights.so_what: Leapfrog dynamics favor nimble small caps in AI adoption; legacy infrastructure is a liability, not a moat, in a technology shift.
insights.quote: It's the the switching cost for a large cap that that has some infrastructure that's just like hardwired in there. It would be like, you know, building a new subway in like Rome.

insights.claim: Mega-cap AI spenders like Amazon and Meta have shifted from piling up cash to becoming effectively cash-flow-negative from AI capex, and if the industry overbuilds capacity at low ROI, the value accrues to the users of AI rather than the builders.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kenny
insights.so_what: If AI capacity is overbuilt, downstream adopters capture the surplus while hyperscaler builders bear the depreciation; position away from the capex-heavy builders.
insights.quote: who gets the benefit if you overbuild this capacity and it turns out to have a low ROI? All the users of it are the one where where all the value would accrue to.

insights.claim: AI-linked 'picks and shovels' industrial names have already re-rated sharply, with an industrial company cited as trading at six times EBITDA before exploding higher as investors saw its total addressable market expand.
insights.speaker: John McNamara
insights.so_what: The first-order AI beneficiary trade in industrial picks-and-shovels has largely played out; the next leg is AI-driven cost reduction in small/mid-caps.
